facts.fact: Long-horizon coding-agent failures often involve error propagation across dependent engineering steps.
Locally reasonable actions can still produce global drift when intermediate work is not checked against the user's intent.
Specification gaps and hidden constraints make it difficult to judge whether a coding-agent patch satisfies real requirements.
Passing visible tests is an insufficient completion signal because tests may miss semantic correctness, architectural fit, or intended behavior.
Uncertainty recognition and escalation are needed when ambiguity makes continued autonomous editing risky.
Accumulated drift, underspecification, and weak verification signals interact, so long-horizon coding agents need verification and clarification checkpoints.
LLM agents must preserve task-relevant state while handling accumulated conversation history, tool outputs, plans, errors, and observations.
Stale assumptions and irrelevant context can pollute later decisions by distracting from the constraints that currently matter.
Context compression can lose later-important details such as exact constraints, failed attempts, file paths, or decision assumptions.
Summaries can preserve the broad goal while losing debugging evidence needed to diagnose a specific failure.
Retrieval can introduce irrelevant, outdated, duplicated, or superficially related information into the agent context.
Effective context management requires keep, discard, and re-check decisions because more context is not automatically better.
Tool-using agents can fail through invalid arguments, schema misunderstanding, or incorrect tool-call ordering.
A reasonable high-level plan can fail at the tool interface when the intended action does not match the tool's required form.
Syntactically valid tool calls can fail when the runtime environment differs from the agent's assumptions.
Environment-related tool failures can involve dependencies, unavailable files, permissions, paths, stale state, network restrictions, or test mismatches.
Robust agents must diagnose failed tool calls instead of repeating the same command or patching symptoms without finding the root cause.
Tool-use brittleness arises from interactions between intended actions, tool interfaces, runtime environments, and recovery behavior.
